input.background: Assume that the product failures from an assembly line are 5%. Suppose that if a product is defective, a diagnostic test indicates that it is defective 92% of the time, and if the product is good, the test indicates that it is good 96% of the time.
input.question: Calculate the probability that the product is good given that the test indicates defective.

output.answer: Let A = product is good (not defective) A_c = product is defective B = a test indicates that it is good (not defective) B_c = a test indicates it is defective Then P(A)=0.95, P(A_c)=0.05; P(B | A)=0.96, P(B_c | A_c)=0.92 The probability that the product is good given that the test indicates defective is: P(A | B_c) = P(A ∩ B_c) / P(B_c) = [P(B_c | A) * P(A)] / [P(B_c | A) * P(A) + P(B_c | A_c) * P(A_c)] = (0.04 * 0.95) / [(0.04 * 0.95) + (0.92 * 0.05)] = 0.4524

input.background: A sample of 3 units is drawn without replacement from a batch of 15 units, of which two are bad. Let X denote the number of bad units in the sample.
input.question: Determine the probability function of X.

output.answer:
(i) $P(X=0)=\frac{13}{15} \cdot \frac{12}{14} \cdot \frac{11}{13}=\frac{22}{35}$

Let g and b denote good and bad units, respectively. We obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=1) & =P\{g, g, b\}+P\{g, b, g\}+P\{b, g, g\} \\
& =\frac{13}{15} \cdot \frac{12}{14} \cdot \frac{2}{13}+\frac{13}{15} \cdot \frac{2}{14} \cdot \frac{12}{13}+\frac{2}{15} \cdot \frac{13}{14} \cdot \frac{12}{13} \\
& =\frac{12}{35}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly,

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=2) & =P\{g, b, b\}+P\{b, g, b\}+P\{b, b, g\} \\
& =\frac{13}{15} \cdot \frac{2}{14} \cdot \frac{1}{13}+\frac{2}{15} \cdot \frac{13}{14} \cdot \frac{1}{13}+\frac{2}{15} \cdot \frac{1}{14} \cdot \frac{13}{13} \\
& =\frac{1}{35}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the probability function of $X$ is

$$
P(X=x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}
\frac{22}{35} \quad x=0 \\
\frac{12}{35} \quad x=1 \\
\frac{1}{35} \quad x=2
\end{array}\right.
$$

input.question: What is the density function of a continuous random variable Y if it is a monotone function of X?

output.answer:
It is easy to see that if X is a continuous random variable with pdf f and y = g(x) is a continuous monotone function of x, then the random variable Y has a density function h as follows:

h(y) = f(x) |dx/dy|

where x = g^{-1}(y).

input.question: Define the skewness coefficient \( S_c \) of a random variable \( X \) and explain its significance.

output.answer: The skewness coefficient of a random variable \( X \) is a measure of the symmetry of the distribution of \( X \) about its mean value \( \mu \), and is defined as \( S_c=\frac{E(X-\mu)^3}{\sigma^3} \). Skewness is zero for a symmetric distribution, negative for a left-tailed distribution, and positive for a right-tailed distribution. It measures the extent to which the distribution departs from symmetry.

meta_info.chapter: Chapter 1: Basic Probability, Statistics, and Reliability

input.background: The problem involves estimating the standard deviation using the mean range, understanding the skewness and kurtosis coefficients of a random variable, and the concept of the median in a dataset. Skewness measures the symmetry of a distribution, while kurtosis measures the heaviness of the tails of the distribution. The median is the central value in an ordered dataset.
input.question: Define the kurtosis coefficient \( K_c \) of a random variable \( X \) and explain its significance.

output.answer: The kurtosis coefficient of a random variable \( X \) is a measure of how much of the mass of the distribution is contained in the tails, and is defined as \( K_c=\frac{E(X-\mu)^4}{\sigma^4} \). Kurtosis is always positive, and larger values represent heavier tails of the distribution. It serves to differentiate between a flat distribution curve and a sharply peaked curve.

meta_info.chapter: Chapter 1: Basic Probability, Statistics, and Reliability

input.background: The problem involves estimating the standard deviation using the mean range, understanding the skewness and kurtosis coefficients of a random variable, and the concept of the median in a dataset. Skewness measures the symmetry of a distribution, while kurtosis measures the heaviness of the tails of the distribution. The median is the central value in an ordered dataset.
input.question: Explain the concept of the median in a dataset and how it is calculated when \( n \) (the total number of observations) is even.

output.answer: The median is the central point of a series when the data are arranged in order of magnitude, i.e., there are equal numbers of observations greater than and less than the median. When \( n \) is even, it is usual to take the mean of the two central values as the median.

input.background: Consider a high-voltage load-sharing system consisting of a power supply and two transmitters, say A and B, using mechanically tuned magnetrons. Two transmitters are used, each tuning one-half the desired frequency range; however, if one transmitter fails, the other can tune the entire range with a resultant change in the expected time to failure. For this system to work, the power supply and at least one of the transmitters must work. The transmitters operate independently when both are functional. An undetected fault in either of the transmitters causes the system to fail. Transmitter A has constant failure rate λ_A if transmitter B is operating and failure rate λ_A' if transmitter B has failed. Similarly, transmitter B has constant failure rate λ_B if transmitter A is operating and failure rate λ_B' if transmitter A has failed. λ_P and λ_C are the constant failure rates of the power supply and fault coverage, respectively.
input.question: Obtain the reliability function R(t) of the high-voltage load-sharing system with dependent failures.

output.answer: The reliability function of the high-voltage load-sharing system is given as follows: $$ R(t)= e^{-(λ_A+λ_B+λ_P)t} + \frac{λ_A e^{-(λ_P+λ_B')t}}{(λ_A+λ_B+λ_C-λ_B')}\left(1-e^{-(λ_A+λ_B+λ_C-λ_B')t}\right) + \frac{λ_B e^{-(λ_P+λ_A')t}}{(λ_A+λ_B+λ_C-λ_A')}\left(1-e^{-(λ_A+λ_B+λ_C-λ_A')t}\right) $$

meta_info.chapter: Chapter 4： System Reliability Modeling

input.background: Consider a high-voltage load-sharing system consisting of a power supply and two transmitters, say A and B, using mechanically tuned magnetrons. Two transmitters are used, each tuning one-half the desired frequency range; however, if one transmitter fails, the other can tune the entire range with a resultant change in the expected time to failure. For this system to work, the power supply and at least one of the transmitters must work. The transmitters operate independently when both are functional. An undetected fault in either of the transmitters causes the system to fail. Transmitter A has constant failure rate λ_A if transmitter B is operating and failure rate λ_A' if transmitter B has failed. Similarly, transmitter B has constant failure rate λ_B if transmitter A is operating and failure rate λ_B' if transmitter A has failed. λ_P and λ_C are the constant failure rates of the power supply and fault coverage, respectively.
input.question: Obtain the mean time to failure (MTTF) of the high-voltage load-sharing system with dependent failures.

output.answer: The system MTTF is given as follows: $$ \operatorname{MTTF} = \frac{1}{λ_A+λ_B+λ_P} + \frac{λ_A}{(λ_A+λ_B+λ_C-λ_B')} \left(\frac{1}{λ_P+λ_B'} - \frac{1}{λ_A+λ_B+λ_C+λ_P}\right) + \frac{λ_B}{(λ_A+λ_B+λ_C-λ_A')} \left(\frac{1}{λ_P+λ_A'} - \frac{1}{λ_A+λ_B+λ_C+λ_P}\right) $$

input.background: A device consists of $m$ units of type I and $n$ units of type II. The reliability for an assigned time $t$ of each type I unit is $p_1$ and that of a type II unit is $p_2$. The units fail independently of one another. For the device to operate, no less than two out of $m$ type I units and no less than two out of $n$ type II units must operate simultaneously for time $t$.
input.question: Find the reliability of the device.

output.answer: The reliability of the device is $$R=\left[1-\left(1-p_1\right)^m-m p_1\left(1-p_1\right)^{m-1}\right]\left[1-\left(1-p_2\right)^n-n p_2\left(1-p_2\right)^{n-1}\right]$$

input.background: A computer system has an exponential failure time density function: $$f(t)=\frac{1}{9,000} e^{-\frac{t}{9,000}}, t \geq 0$$. The problem asks about the probability of failure within a specific time interval.
input.question: What is the reliability function for an exponential failure time density function?

output.answer: If the time to failure is described by an exponential failure time density function, then $$f(t) = \frac{1}{\theta} e^{-\frac{t}{\theta}}, \quad t \geq 0, \theta > 0$$, and this will lead to the reliability function $$R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\theta} e^{-\frac{s}{\theta}} ds = e^{-\frac{t}{\theta}}, \quad t \geq 0$$.

input.background: A computer system has an exponential failure time density function: $$f(t)=\frac{1}{9,000} e^{-\frac{t}{9,000}}, t \geq 0$$. The problem asks about the probability of failure within a specific time interval.
input.question: What is the reliability function for a Weibull distribution?

output.answer: Consider the Weibull distribution where the failure time density function is given by $$f(t) = \frac{\beta t^{\beta - 1}}{\theta^\beta} e^{-\left( \frac{t}{\theta} \right)^\beta}, \quad t \geq 0, \theta > 0, \beta > 0$$. Then the reliability function is $$R(t) = e^{-\left( \frac{t}{\theta} \right)^\beta}, \quad t \geq 0$$.

input.background: Suppose that 15 units are tested over a 72-h period. Five units failed with one unit each failing after 5, 15, 20, 50, and 68 h. The remaining 10 units have survived at the end of the 72-h test period.
input.question: Estimate the constant failure rate based on the given data.

output.answer:
The total unit operating hours, $T_{\text {total }}$, are:

$$
T_{\text {total }}=(5+15+20+50+68)+10(72)=878 .
$$

Thus,

$$
\lambda=\frac{5 \text { failures }}{878 \text { unit operating hours }}=0.0057 \text { failures per hour. }
$$

In other words, on the average about $0.57 \%$ of the units would be expected to fail in a one-hour period. Furthermore, over a $72-\mathrm{h}$ period, about $(0.0057 * 72)=0.41$ or $41 \%$ of the units would be expected to fail. In the actual test given above, only $33.33 \%$ failed (i.e., $(5)(100) / 15=33.33$ ).

input.background: Suppose that 15 units are tested over a 72-h period. Five units failed with one unit each failing after 5, 15, 20, 50, and 68 h. The remaining 10 units have survived at the end of the 72-h test period.
input.question: Explain the relationship between the hazard rate function, the probability density function, and the reliability function.

output.answer:
The death rate, in statistical theory, is analogous to the failure rate as the force of mortality is analogous to the hazard rate function. Therefore, the hazard rate function or failure rate function is the ratio of the probability density function (pdf) to the reliability function. In reliability engineering, the survival function is often referred to as the reliability function. In actuarial science, the hazard function is known as the force of mortality. By definition, a mathematical reliability function is the probability that a system will be successful in the interval from time 0 to time t, given by

$$
R(t)=\int_t^{\infty} f(s) d s=e^{-\int_0^t h(s) d s}
$$

where f (s) and h(s) are, respectively, the failure time density and failure rate function.

input.background: A manufacturer has observed that over the course of 10 months, the numbers of products demanded were $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_{10}$, respectively. If we assume that these are i.i.d. observations of demand.
input.question: What bound can be put on the probability that their average $\bar{T}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{10} T_i}{10}$ is more than $3 \sigma$ products from the average demand $\mu$?

output.answer:
We obtain

$$
E(\bar{T})=\mu, \quad \operatorname{Var}(\bar{T})=\frac{\sigma^2}{10}
$$

From the Chebyshev's inequality,

$$
P\{|\bar{T}-\mu| \geq 3 \sigma\}=P\left\{\left|\frac{\bar{T}-\mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{10}}\right| \geq 3 \sqrt{10}\right\} \leq \frac{1}{(3 \sqrt{10})^2}=\frac{1}{90}
$$

input.background: A life insurance company wants to predict the number of claims it may receive in the coming year in a particular town due to severe weather such as floods. There are currently 250 active flood insurance policies in the town, and the company estimates the probability of any flood occurring in the coming year is 0.002. The claims are assumed to be independent.
input.question: Determine the probability that the insurance company receives more than 4 claims in this town out of 250 active policies assuming that the claims are independent.

output.answer:
Let $N$ be the number of claims in the coming year. Then $N \sim$ binomial $(250, p=0.002)$. Then the desired probability is given by

$$
P(N>4)=1-\sum_{n=0}^4\binom{250}{n}(0.002)^n(0.998)^{250-n}=1.6664*10^{-4}
$$

input.background: Assume there are 10 identical units to place on test at the same time t = 0 where each unit has an exponential life distribution with a mean time to failure MTTF μ = 5 h and that the units operate independently.
input.question: Determine the expected and the variance of number of failures during the first hour, i.e. interval [0, 1).

output.answer: Let $N_1$ be the number of failures during the first hour, in the time interval $[0$, 1). The probability of failure of a unit in the time interval $[0,1)$ is $$ q_1=1-e^{- rac{1}{5}}=0.1813 $$ The distribution of $N_1$ that the probability of exactly $i$ failures in $[0,1)$ is $$ P\left(N_1=i\right)=\binom{10}{i}(0.1813)^i(0.8187)^{10-i} $$ The expected number of failures $\left(N_1\right)$ during the first hour is $$ E\left(N_1\right)=n q_1=10(0.1813)=1.813 $$ The variance of $N_1$ is $$ \begin{aligned} V\left(N_1\right) & =n q_1\left(1-q_1\right) \\ & =10(0.1813)(0.8187)=1.4843 \end{aligned} $$

input.background: Assume there are 10 identical units to place on test at the same time t = 0 where each unit has an exponential life distribution with a mean time to failure MTTF μ = 5 h and that the units operate independently.
input.question: What is the expected and the variance of the number of failures during the interval [1, 2).

output.answer: Let $N_2$ be the number of failures during the second hour, in the time interval $[1,2)$. Assume that the unit will not fail during the first hour, the probability of failure of a unit in the time interval $[1,2)$ is $$ \begin{aligned} q_2 & =P(1<T \leq 2)=P(T \leq 2)-P(T \leq 1) \\ & =\left(1-e^{-\frac{2}{5}}\right)-\left(1-e^{-\frac{1}{5}}\right)=0.1484 \end{aligned} $$ The distribution of $N_2$ that the probability of exactly $i$ failures during the time interval $[1,2)$ is $$ P\left(N_2=i\right)=\binom{10}{i}(0.1484)^i(0.8516)^{10-i} $$ The expected number of failures $\left(N_2\right)$ during the interval $[1,2)$ is $$ E\left(N_2\right)=n q_2=10(0.1484)=1.484 $$ The variance of $\mathrm{N}_2$ is $$ \begin{aligned} V\left(N_2\right) & =n q_2\left(1-q_2\right) \\ & =10(0.1484)(0.8516)=1.2638 \end{aligned} $$

input.background: An electronic manufacturer produces air cooling units for computer systems. There is a 3% chance that any given cooling unit is defective, independent of the other units produced.
input.question: Determine the probability that more than 2 units in a shipment of 100 are defective.

output.answer: Let X be the number of defectives in a shipment of 100, then X has a binomial distribution with n = 100 and p = 0.97, i.e., X ~ b(100, 0.97) where p is the probability of a non-defective unit. The desired probability is given by $$\begin{aligned} P(X>2) & =1-P(X \leq 2) \\ & =1-\sum_{i=0}^2\binom{100}{\mathrm{i}}(0.03)^i(0.97)^{100-i} \\ & =0.5802 \end{aligned}$$

input.background: An entrepreneur of a start-up company calls potential customers to sell a new product. Assume that the outcomes of consecutive calls are independent and that on each call he has a 20% chance of making a sale. His daily goal is to make 5 sales, and he can make only 25 calls in a day.
input.question: What is the probability that he achieves his goal in 15 trials?

output.answer:
We wish to find the probability that his fifth sale (success) will be at trial 15. Note that in this case, the number of successes r=5 is fixed. Thus the number of calls is random, and we have the negative binomial distribution. Here p=0.20. Let Y be the number of trials up to and including the fifth success. Therefore, from the negative binomial distribution below:

P(Y=n) = (n-1 choose r-1) * p^r * (1-p)^(n-r), where (n choose r) = n! / (r!(n-r)!)

We can obtain:

P(Y=15) = (14 choose 4) * (0.2)^5 * (0.8)^10 = 0.0344

input.background: An entrepreneur of a start-up company calls potential customers to sell a new product. Assume that the outcomes of consecutive calls are independent and that on each call he has a 20% chance of making a sale. His daily goal is to make 5 sales, and he can make only 25 calls in a day.
input.question: What is the probability that he achieves his goal in between 15 and 20 trials?

output.answer:
Let S_n be the number of successes in n trials. Here the number of trials is fixed (i.e., n=20). We can then use the binomial distribution. The entrepreneur does not achieve his daily goal if S_n ≤ 2. Thus:

P(S_20 ≤ 2) = Σ P(S_20 = x) for x=0 to 2
= (20 choose 0) * (0.2)^0 * (0.8)^20 + (20 choose 1) * (0.2)^1 * (0.8)^19 + (20 choose 2) * (0.2)^2 * (0.8)^18
= 0.1969

input.background: A nuclear plant is located in an area susceptible to both high winds and earthquakes. From the historical data, the mean frequency of large earthquakes capable of damaging important plant structures is one every 50 years. The corresponding frequency of damaging high winds is once in 25 years. During a strong earthquake, the probability of structure damage is 0.1. During high winds, the damage probability is 0.05. Assume that earthquakes and high winds can be described by independent Poisson random variables and that the damages caused by these events are independent.
input.question: What is the probability of having strong winds but not large earthquakes during a 10-year period?

output.answer: Let the random variable $X$ and $Y$ represent the number of earthquakes and the number of occurrences of high winds, respectively. We assume that the two random variables are statistically independent. The mean of $X$ and $Y$ are, respectively, given by $$\lambda_X=\frac{1}{50 \ \text { years }}(10 \ \text { years })=0.2$$ and $$\lambda_Y=\frac{1}{25 \ \text { year }}(10 \ \text { years })=0.4$$. The conditional damage probabilities are given as follows: $$P(\ \text { damage/earthquake })=0.1$$ and $$P(\ \text { damage } / \ \text { wind })=0.05$$. Let the event $A=\{$ strong winds and no earthquakes $\}$. Assuming that winds and earthquakes are independent, the probability of having strong winds but not earthquakes during the 10-year period can be written as $$\begin{aligned} P(A) & =P(w \ \text { inds }) P(\ \text { no earthquakes }) \\ & =[1-P(\ \text { no winds })] P(\ \text { no earthquakes }) \end{aligned}$$. Therefore, we obtain $$P(A)=\left(1-e^{-0.4}\right)\left(e^{-0.2}\right)=0.27$$.

meta_info.chapter: Chapter 2: Distribution Functions and Its Applications

input.background: A nuclear plant is located in an area susceptible to both high winds and earthquakes. From the historical data, the mean frequency of large earthquakes capable of damaging important plant structures is one every 50 years. The corresponding frequency of damaging high winds is once in 25 years. During a strong earthquake, the probability of structure damage is 0.1. During high winds, the damage probability is 0.05. Assume that earthquakes and high winds can be described by independent Poisson random variables and that the damages caused by these events are independent.
input.question: What is the probability of having strong winds and large earthquakes in the 10-year period?

output.answer: The probability of having strong winds and earthquakes during the 10-year period can be obtained $$\begin{aligned} P(B) & =P(\ \text { winds }) P(\ \text { earthquakes }) \\ & =[1-P(\ \text { no winds })][1-P(\ \text { no earthquakes })] \\ & =\left(1-e^{-0.4}\right)\left(1-e^{-0.2}\right)=0.06 \end{aligned}$$.

input.background: A nuclear plant is located in an area susceptible to both high winds and earthquakes. From the historical data, the mean frequency of large earthquakes capable of damaging important plant structures is one every 50 years. The corresponding frequency of damaging high winds is once in 25 years. During a strong earthquake, the probability of structure damage is 0.1. During high winds, the damage probability is 0.05. Assume that earthquakes and high winds can be described by independent Poisson random variables and that the damages caused by these events are independent.
input.question: What is the probability of building damage during the 10-year period?

output.answer: We assume that there will not be multiple occurrences of earthquakes and high winds during the 10-year period. Therefore, the probability of building damage can be written as $$\begin{aligned} P(C) & =P(\ \text { damage/earthquakes }) P(\ \text { earthquakes }) \\ & +\mathrm{P}(\ \text { damage/wind }) \mathrm{P}(\ \text { wind }) \\ & -\mathrm{P}(\ \text { damage/earthquakes and wind }) \mathrm{P}(\ \text { earthquake and wind }) \\ & =P(\ \text { damage/earthquakes }) P(\ \text { earthquakes }) \\ & +\mathrm{P}(\ \text { damage/wind }) \mathrm{P}(\ \text { wind }) \\ & -\mathrm{P}(\ \text { damage/earthquakes }) \mathrm{P}(\ \text { damage/wind }) \mathrm{P}(\ \text { earthquake and wind }) \\ & =\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-0.2}\right)(0.1)+\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-0.4}\right)(0.05)-(0.05)(0.1)(0.06) \\ & =0.0343 \end{aligned}$$.

input.background: The major accident of hospitalization tends to vary with the snow storms over the winter season of a given year. Data from an ambulance emergency center suggests that one should expect 2 hospitalizations in October, 3 hospitalizations in November, 4 hospitalizations in December, and 6 hospitalizations in January. Assume that hospitalizations are independent.
input.question: Determine the probability that there are more than 10 hospitalizations over a period from October to January.

output.answer:
Let $X_1$ be the number of hospitalizations in October, $X_2$ be the number of hospitalizations in November, $X_3$ be the number of hospitalizations in December, and $X_4$ be the number of hospitalizations in January. Let $N$ be the total number of hospitalizations over a period from October to January, then.

$$
X_1 \sim \text { Poisson (2), } X_2 \sim \text { Poisson (3), } X_3 \sim \text { Poisson (4), } X_4 \sim \text { Poisson (6). }
$$

and so,

$$
N=X_1+X_2+X_3+X_4 \sim \text { Poisson }(2+3+4+6=15) .
$$

Then we obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(N>10) & =1-P(N \leq 10) \\
& =1-\sum_{n=0}^{10} \frac{(15)^n e^{-15}}{n!}=0.883
\end{aligned}
$$

input.background: At busy time a telephone exchange is very near capacity, so callers have difficulty placing their calls. It may be of interest to know the number of attempts necessary in order to gain a connection. Suppose that we let p=0.05 be the probability of a connection during busy time.
input.question: What is the probability that 4 attempts are necessary for a successful call?

output.answer: The probability that four attempts are necessary for a successful call is: $$P(X=4)=p(1-p)^3=(0.05)(1-0.05)^3=0.0429$$

input.background: The probability of failure of a medical device when it is tested is equal to 0.05. Assume independence of tests.
input.question: How can the memoryless property of the geometric distribution be shown?

output.answer: This memoryless property can be shown as follows. We know that $$P(N>n)=P(N \geq n+1)=(1-p)^n$$ Thus, $$\begin{aligned} P(N \geq(n+s) \mid N>s) & =\frac{P(N \geq n+s)}{P(N>s)} \\ & =\frac{(1-p)^{n+s-1}}{(1-p)^s} \\ & =(1-p)^{n-1} \\ & =P(N \geq n) \end{aligned}$$

output.answer: Let X be the number of defective items in a sample of size 20, then it has a hypergeometric distribution and its probability function is as follows: $$ P(X=x)=\frac{\binom{25}{x}\binom{475}{20-x}}{\binom{500}{20}} \quad x=0,1,2, \ldots, 20 $$

output.answer: The hazard function or failure rate for the exponential density function is constant, i.e., $$h(t)=\frac{f(t)}{R(t)}=\frac{\frac{1}{\theta} e^{-\frac{1}{\theta}}}{e^{-\frac{1}{\theta}}}=\frac{1}{\theta}=\lambda$$ The failure rate for this distribution is $\lambda$, a constant, which is the main reason for this widely used distribution. Because of its constant failure rate property, the exponential is an excellent model for the long flat.

input.background: The failure time of a certain component has a Weibull distribution with $\beta=4, \mu=2000$, and $\sigma=1000$.
input.question: Explain how the Rayleigh and exponential distributions are special cases of the Weibull distribution.

output.answer: Note that the Rayleigh and exponential distributions are special cases of the Weibull distribution at $\beta=2, \gamma=0$, and $\beta=1, \gamma=0$, respectively. For example, when $\beta=1$ and $\gamma=0$, the reliability of the Weibull distribution function in Eq. (2.69) reduces to $$ R(t)=e^{-\frac{t}{\theta}} $$ and the hazard function given in Eq. (2.70) reduces to $1 / \theta$, a constant. Thus, the exponential is a special case of the Weibull distribution. Similarly, when $\beta=2$ and $\theta=\sqrt{2} \alpha$, the two-parameter Weibull distribution becomes the Rayleigh density function as follows: $$ f(t)=\frac{t}{\alpha^2} e^{-\frac{t^2}{2 \alpha^2}} \quad t \geq 0 $$

output.answer:
In general, for $\mathrm{a}<\mathrm{b}$ we can obtain

$$
P(a \leq X \leq b) \simeq \Phi\left(\frac{b+1 / 2-n p}{\sqrt{n p(1-p)}}\right)-\Phi\left(\frac{a-1 / 2-n p}{\sqrt{n p(1-p)}}\right)
$$

where $X$ is binomial $\mathrm{b}(n, p)$ with the expected value of $X$ being $n p$ for large $n$.

input.background: Consider a sequence of 25 Bernoulli trials (binomial situation) where each trial results in either success or failure. From the 25 trials, 6 failures and 19 successes result. Let p be the probability of success, and 1-p the probability of failure. The task is to find the estimator of p, denoted as \hat{p}, which maximizes the likelihood of the observed outcome. Additionally, the problem introduces the general concept and methodology of Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), which is used to estimate parameters by maximizing the likelihood function given the observed data. The methodology is described in detail, including steps for deriving MLEs and their properties.
input.question: Find the estimator of p, \hat{p}, that maximizes the likelihood of observing 19 successes and 6 failures in 25 Bernoulli trials.

output.answer: The sample density function can be written as $$ g(19)=\binom{25}{19} p^{19}(1-p)^6 $$ The maximum of g(19) occurs when $$ p=\hat{p}=\frac{19}{25} $$ so that $$ g\left(19 \left\lvert\, p=\frac{19}{25}\right.\right) \geq g\left(19 \left\lvert\, p \neq \frac{19}{25}\right.\right) $$ Thus, \hat{p} is the maximum likelihood estimate of p.

input.question: Describe the general methodology of Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for estimating parameters in a probability distribution.

output.answer: The principle of Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), originally developed by R. A. Fisher in the 1920s, states that the desired probability distribution is the one that makes the observed data 'most likely.' This involves finding the parameter vector that maximizes the likelihood function. The steps for obtaining the MLE are: Step 1. Find the joint density function L(X, \theta). Step 2. Take the natural log of the joint density, \ln L(\theta). Step 3. Take the partial derivatives of \ln L(\theta) (or L(\theta)) with respect to each parameter. Step 4. Set partial derivatives to 'zero.' Step 5. Solve for parameter(s).

input.question: Explain the mathematical formulation of the likelihood function and its maximization process.

output.answer: Consider a random sample X_1, X_2, ..., X_n from a distribution with pdf f(x; \theta). Assuming the random variables are independent, the likelihood function is given by $$ L(X, \theta)=\prod_{i=1}^n f(X_i; \theta) $$ The maximum likelihood estimator \hat{\theta} is found by maximizing L(X; \theta) with respect to \theta. In practice, it is often easier to maximize \ln[L(X; \theta)] because the logarithm turns the multiplication of terms into addition. The log likelihood function is given by $$ \ln L(\theta)=\sum_{i=1}^n \ln f(X_i; \theta) $$ The MLE \hat{\theta} satisfies $$ \frac{\partial[\ln L(X; \theta)]}{\partial \theta_i}=0 \text{ for } i=1,2,...,m $$ These equations are solved to find the estimates. Care must be taken to ensure the solution is a maximum and not a minimum or inflection point.

input.background: Consider a sequence of 25 Bernoulli trials (binomial situation) where each trial results in either success or failure. From the 25 trials, 6 failures and 19 successes result. input.question: Discuss the properties of the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) and its asymptotic behavior.

output.answer: The MLE \hat{\theta} is consistent and asymptotically efficient, with a limiting distribution given by $$ \sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta}-\theta_0) \rightarrow N(0, I^{-1}(\theta_0)) $$ where \theta_0 is the true parameter value and I is the Fisher information matrix. The asymptotic variance matrix I^{-1}(\theta_0) can be estimated consistently by an empirical variance matrix of the influence functions evaluated at \hat{\theta}.

input.background: A random sample of 395 people were surveyed. Each person was asked their interest in riding a bicycle (Variable A) and their age (Variable B). The data that resulted from the survey is summarized in Table 3.10.
input.question: Is there evidence to conclude, at the 0.05 level, that the desire to ride a bicycle depends on age? In other words, is age independent of the desire to ride a bicycle?

output.answer:
Here $r=2$ and $c=4$. The expected number in each cell can be obtained as shown in Table 3.11:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E_{11}=\frac{1}{395}\left(\sum_{i=1}^2 O_{i 1}\right)\left(\sum_{j=1}^4 O_{1 j}\right)=\frac{1}{395}(100)(201)=50.8861 \
& E_{12}=\frac{1}{395}\left(\sum_{i=1}^2 O_{i 2}\right)\left(\sum_{j=1}^4 O_{1 j}\right)=\frac{1}{395}(98)(201)=49.8684 \
& E_{13}=\frac{1}{395}(99)(201)=50.3772 \
& \ldots \
& E_{23}=\frac{1}{395}(99)(194)=48.6228 \
& E_{24}=\frac{1}{395}(98)(194)=48.1316
\end{aligned}
$$

The test statistic value

$$
\lambda_{v a l u e}^2=\sum_{i=1}^2 \sum_{j=1}^4 \frac{\left(O_{i j}-E_{i j}\right)^2}{E_{i j}}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{(60-50.8861)^2}{50.8861}+\frac{(54-49.8684)^2}{49.8684}+\ldots+\frac{(57-48.1316)^2}{48.1316} \
& =8.0062
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $\alpha=0.05$. So $\chi_{0.05,(2-1)(4-1)}^2=\chi_{0.05,3}^2=7.815$. Since $\chi_{\text {value }}^2=8.0062>$ 7.815 we, therefore, reject the null hypothesis. That is, there is sufficient evidence, at the 0.05 level, to conclude that the desire to ride a bicycle depends on age.

input.question: What is the failure time density of the system?

output.answer: The system failure time density is $$f(t)=\frac{d F}{d t}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(t) \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} F_j(t)$$.

input.background: A parallel system consists of 3 independent components, each having 0.95 reliability. The system reliability is calculated using the formula $$R=1-(1-0.95)^3=0.9999$$.

output.answer: The system failure rate can be obtained as $$r(t)=\frac{f(t)}{R(t)}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(t) \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} F_j(t)}{1-\prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}} F_j(t)}$$.

output.answer: If each of the $n$ independent components has an exponential failure time distribution with constant failure rate $\lambda_i$, the system reliability is $$R(t)=1-\prod_{i=1}^n\left(1-e^{-\lambda_i t}\right)$$.

meta_info.chapter: Chapter 4： System Reliability Modeling

input.background: A parallel system consists of 3 independent components, each having 0.95 reliability. The system reliability is calculated using the formula $$R=1-(1-0.95)^3=0.9999$$.
input.question: How can the mean time to failure (MTTF) of the system be calculated?

output.answer: The mean time to failure of the system can be obtained as $$M_{tbf}=\int_0^{\infty} R(t) dt=\int_0^{\infty}\left(1-\prod_{i=1}^n\left(1-e^{-\lambda_i t}\right)\right) dt$$. After simplifications, $$M_{tbf}=\sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j_i=1}^n \sum_{\substack{j_{i-1}=1 \\ \left\{j_1>j_2>\ldots>j_i\right\}}}^n \ldots \sum_{j_2=1}^n \sum_{j_1=1}^n \frac{(-1)^{i+1}}{\left(\sum_{k=1}^i \lambda_{j_k}\right)}$$.

meta_info.chapter: Chapter 4： System Reliability Modeling

input.question: What is the MTTF for a 2-component parallel system?

output.answer: For $n=2$, the system MTTF is $$M_{tbf}=\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{1}{\lambda_2}-\frac{1}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}$$.

input.background: Consider a standby system with two identical valves each with constant failure rate $\lambda=10^{-5}$ failures/hours. The probability $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}$ that the switch $S$ will activate the standby valve is 0.995.
input.question: What is the reliability of the system when the components are non-identical, with component 1 (active unit) and component 2 (standby unit) having constant failure rates $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, respectively, and $p_s$ being the probability of successful switching?

output.answer: From Eq. (4.45), the reliability is $$\begin{aligned} R(t) & =e^{-\lambda_1 t}+p_s \int_0^t \lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_1 \ \tau} e^{-\lambda_2(t-\ \tau)} d \ \tau \\ & =e^{-\lambda_1 t}+\frac{p_s \lambda_1}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2} e^{-\lambda_2 t}-\frac{p_s \lambda_1}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2} e^{-\lambda_1 t} \end{aligned}$$

input.background: A system is composed of eight identical active power supplies, at least seven of the eight are required for the system to function. When two of the eight power supplies fail, the system fails. Each power supply has a constant failure rate λa per hour when all eight are operating. If one power supply fails, the remaining power supplies have a failure rate λb per hour where λa ≤ λb. A failed power supply can be repaired with a constant rate μ per hour. The system reliability function, R(t), is defined as the probability that the system continues to function throughout the interval (0, t). The goal is to determine the system mean time to failure (MTTF). The system is modeled using three states: State 0 (all 8 units working), State 1 (7 units working), and State 2 (more than one unit failed and system does not work). Initial conditions are P0(0)=1, P1(0)=P2(0)=0. Differential equations are provided to model the system behavior.
input.question: Write the differential equations for the Markov model of the system.

output.answer:
The differential equations are:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P_0^{\prime}(t)=-8 \lambda_a P_0(t)+\mu P_1(t) \\
& P_1^{\prime}(t)=8 \lambda_a P_0(t)-\left(7 \lambda_b+\mu\right) P_1(t) \\
& P_2^{\prime}(t)=7 \lambda_b P_1(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

input.background: Suppose that a system is restored to 'as good as new' periodically at intervals of time T. The system renews itself at time T, 2T, ..., and the system reliability under preventive maintenance (PM) is defined as R_M(t) = Pr{failure has not occurred by time t}. The system survives to time t if and only if it survives every PM cycle {1, 2, ..., k} and a further time (t-kT).
input.question: For a system with a failure time probability density function f(t) = λ^2 t e^(-λ t) for t > 0, λ > 0, obtain the system reliability under PM and system mean time to first failure.

output.answer: The system reliability under PM is R_M(t) = [R(T)]^k R(t-kT) for kT < t < (k+1)T and k = 0, 1, 2, .... Given the probability density function f(t) = λ^2 t e^(-λ t), the reliability function is R(t) = ∫_t^∞ f(x) dx = (1 + λt)e^(-λt). Thus, R_M(t) = [(1 + λT)e^(-λT)]^k [(1 + λ(t-kT))e^(-λ(t-kT))]. The system mean time to first failure (MTTFF) is MTTFF = ∫_0^∞ R_M(t) dt = ∑_k=0^∞ [R(T)]^k ∫_0^T R(u) du = (∫_0^T R(u) du) / (1 - R(T)). From R(u), ∫_0^T R(x) dx = (2/λ)(1 - e^(-λT)) - T e^(-λT). Thus, MTTFF = [(2/λ)(1 - e^(-λT)) - T e^(-λT)] / [1 - (1 + λT)e^(-λT)].

$$

where $0<\beta<1$.
